content.text: Distributed tracing captures the full journey of a request across every service it touches, producing a

— a tree of spans that records latency, errors, and context at each hop. In any real system, the volume of traces is proportional to traffic. At even moderate scale, retaining every trace is prohibitively expensive: a service handling 10 000 requests per second generates tens of millions of spans per minute. Sampling is therefore unavoidable; the question is

Tail-based sampling (TBS) defers that decision until after a trace is complete — specifically, after the root span closes and all child spans have been received by a central collector. The collector buffers every span belonging to a trace, evaluates the fully assembled trace against a set of policies (error status, latency thresholds, endpoint patterns, user-defined rules), and then either retains the entire trace or discards it atomically. This is the fundamental difference from
, where the sampling decision is made at the very start of a request — before any outcome is known.

Head sampling discards traces blindly. A 1-in-100 probabilistic head sampler will, on average, keep 1% of error traces, 1% of slow traces, and 1% of traces that expose race conditions — the exact traces an on-call engineer needs. When an incident fires, the storage system may have no trace at all for the failing request because it was dropped at ingress 30 minutes ago. Tail-based sampling solves this by making the decision when the outcome is known: errors are kept with 100% probability, latency outliers are kept, and only uninteresting successful fast requests are probabilistically discarded.

For agentic AI systems the stakes are higher still. A single agent invocation may fan out into dozens of LLM calls, tool executions, and retrieval steps. Debugging a silent failure — wrong tool called, context window overflow, unexpected refusal — requires the complete trace. Losing it to head sampling means the failure is invisible. Tail sampling ensures that the traces most likely to be consulted (errors, regressions, latency spikes) are always present in the backend.
Error-status policies guarantee that every trace containing a failing span is kept, regardless of traffic volume. Root-cause analysis is never blocked by a missing trace.
Latency threshold policies capture the slow tail of the distribution — the p99 and p999 requests that drive SLA breaches — while discarding the cheap majority of fast requests.
By aggressively dropping healthy traces while retaining interesting ones, teams typically achieve 90–99% storage reduction at high throughput, without sacrificing debuggability.
Multiple policies combine via AND/OR logic. A composite policy can keep traces that are EITHER slow OR errored OR match a specific TraceQL filter, applying different sampling rates to each class.
Unlike head sampling, which can keep some spans and discard others from the same trace (producing broken waterfall views), tail sampling keeps or drops a trace in its entirety.

The collector must buffer all spans for every in-flight trace until the root span arrives. At 50 000 spans/second with a 30-second TTL, that is 1.5 million spans held in RAM simultaneously. Under-provisioned collectors OOM and lose entire batches.

All spans belonging to the same traceId must arrive at the same collector instance. Load balancers must route by traceId (not round-robin), requiring sticky routing or a load-balancing collector tier in front of the tail samplers.

Spans are not forwarded to the tracing backend until the decision is made — a delay equal to the trace duration plus the TTL buffer window (typically 5–30 seconds). Real-time trace search is not possible during this window.

A tail sampling collector failure loses all buffered spans. It must be treated with the same availability posture as the services it observes — redundancy, health checks, and graceful drain on shutdown are mandatory.

Sampling policies require ongoing maintenance. An aggressive rate-limit policy that was appropriate at last year's traffic level may under-sample today, silently discarding errors if the threshold is reached too early.
In head-based sampling, the SDK at the entry point (the first service to receive a request) flips a coin — keep with probability
, drop otherwise. The sampling decision is encoded in the trace context header (

flags field) and propagated to every downstream service. Every service honors the upstream decision: if the root said drop, no span is recorded anywhere. This is extremely cheap — the decision is O(1) and the downstream services pay zero telemetry cost — but it is uninformed. At the moment the coin is flipped, the outcome of the request is unknown.
Tail-based sampling inverts this. Every service emits every span regardless of outcome, sending them to a collector. The collector groups spans by

into an in-memory buffer. When the root span arrives (signalling that the request has completed), the collector assembles the complete trace and evaluates it against configured policies. The decision — keep or drop — is made once for the entire trace, and either the full trace is forwarded to the backend or every buffered span is discarded.
the fallback probabilistic rate for healthy traces. The effective storage rate is:

attrs.latex: R_{\text{stored}} = E + L + p \cdot (N - E - L)

content.text: At 10 000 traces/second with E = 50, L = 100, and p = 0.01, tail sampling stores 50 + 100 + 0.01 × 9850 = 248.5 traces/second — a 97.5% reduction — while retaining every error and every slow trace. Head sampling at 1% would store 100 traces/second and miss approximately 98.5% of errors.

Spans arrive at the collector in arbitrary order — a child span from a slow downstream service may arrive before its parent. The collector holds all spans for a traceId in a map keyed by traceId. A configurable TTL (commonly 10–30 seconds) determines how long a trace is buffered before being force-evaluated, even if the root span has not yet arrived. This handles long-running traces and cases where the root span is lost due to a crash. When the TTL fires without a root span, the collector evaluates the partial trace and typically applies a conservative policy (keep it, since a missing root span often indicates a failure).

The diagram below shows the two-tier collector pattern: a load-balancing layer routes spans by traceId to the correct tail sampling collector instance, which buffers and evaluates them before forwarding kept traces to the tracing backend.

attrs.alt: Tail-based sampling architecture: services emit spans to a tail sampling collector that buffers by traceId, evaluates policies, and keeps or drops whole traces before forwarding to the tracing backend

content.text: The two-tier design is important. A

at the front receives all spans from all services and routes them to the correct tail-sampling collector instance by hashing the traceId. This ensures all spans for a given trace land on the same instance. The tail-sampling collectors buffer, evaluate, and forward. Without traceId-aware routing, spans for the same trace would scatter across instances, making complete-trace assembly impossible.
Trace volume exceeds what the backend can store affordably at 100% retention (generally above ~1 000 traces/second).
Errors are rare relative to total traffic, and losing any error trace creates a blind spot during incidents.
SLO compliance requires verifiable evidence of latency distributions, requiring the p95/p99 outliers to always be present.
The system involves agentic loops or multi-step AI workflows where failures are silent and the only debug signal is the full trace.
Do not use tail sampling when
Trace volume is low enough that 100% retention is affordable. Tail sampling adds operational complexity that is not justified at low volumes.
The collector tier cannot be made stateful and highly available. A stateless collector environment (ephemeral serverless collectors) is incompatible with tail sampling's buffering requirement.
Traces are extremely long-running (minutes or hours). Buffering a one-hour trace in RAM is not practical; head sampling or remote-write approaches are more appropriate.
Real-time trace visibility during the request is required (the buffer delay makes this impossible).
Configure a front-tier OTel Collector with the
exporter, which hashes traceIds and routes to a consistent set of backend collector endpoints. Each backend endpoint is a tail-sampling collector instance.

# load-balancer-collector.yaml
receivers:
  otlp:
    protocols:
      grpc:
        endpoint: 0.0.0.0:4317
      http:
        endpoint: 0.0.0.0:4318

exporters:
  loadbalancing:
    protocol:
      otlp:
        tls:
          insecure: true
    resolver:
      static:
        hostnames:
          - tail-sampler-0:4317
          - tail-sampler-1:4317
          - tail-sampler-2:4317
    # Route by traceId (default) ensures all spans for a trace
    # always reach the same tail-sampling instance
    routing_key: "traceId"

service:
  pipelines:
    traces:
      receivers: [otlp]
      exporters: [loadbalancing]
Step 2: Configure the tail sampling processor
Each tail-sampling collector instance runs the
processor from the OpenTelemetry Collector Contrib distribution. The processor buffers spans in memory and evaluates policies when the root span arrives or the TTL expires.

# tail-sampler-collector.yaml
receivers:
  otlp:
    protocols:
      grpc:
        endpoint: 0.0.0.0:4317

processors:
  tail_sampling:
    # How long to wait for the root span before force-evaluating.
    # Must be >= max expected trace duration.
    decision_wait: 30s
    # Max concurrent traces held in memory.
    num_traces: 50000
    # Expected throughput hint for internal channel sizing.
    expected_new_traces_per_sec: 1000

    policies:
      # Policy 1: Always keep traces with any error span
      - name: error-traces
        type: status_code
        status_code:
          status_codes: [ERROR]

      # Policy 2: Keep slow traces (any span > 500ms)
      - name: slow-traces
        type: latency
        latency:
          threshold_ms: 500

      # Policy 3: Rate-limit the total kept traces to protect storage
      - name: rate-limiter
        type: rate_limiting
        rate_limiting:
          spans_per_second: 2000

      # Policy 4: Sample 1% of remaining healthy, fast traces
      - name: probabilistic-baseline
        type: probabilistic
        probabilistic:
          sampling_percentage: 1

exporters:
  otlp:
    endpoint: tempo:4317
    tls:
      insecure: true

service:
  pipelines:
    traces:
      receivers: [otlp]
      processors: [tail_sampling]
      exporters: [otlp]
This minimal configuration keeps all error traces and 0.5% of healthy traces. It is a sensible starting point for a medium-traffic API service before more specific policies are calibrated.

processors:
  tail_sampling:
    decision_wait: 10s
    num_traces: 10000
    expected_new_traces_per_sec: 200
    policies:
      - name: keep-errors
        type: status_code
        status_code:
          status_codes: [ERROR]
      - name: keep-sample
        type: probabilistic
        probabilistic:
          sampling_percentage: 0.5
Example 2: Grafana Tempo native tail sampling with TraceQL

Grafana Tempo (v2.3+) supports native tail-based sampling configured directly in the Tempo server, using TraceQL expressions as policy filters. This eliminates the need for a separate OTel Collector tier for the decision logic, though a load-balancing ingestion tier is still required to route traces to the correct Tempo distributor shard.

# tempo.yaml — tail sampling configuration block
tail_sampling:
  enabled: true
  # Buffer window before evaluating the assembled trace
  decision_wait: 30s
  policies:
    # Keep all traces where any span has status=error
    - policy_type: status_code
      status_code:
        status_codes: [ERROR]

    # Keep traces where root span duration > 1 second
    - policy_type: latency
      latency:
        threshold_ms: 1000

    # TraceQL policy: keep any trace touching the payments service
    # that is also slow (span-level and trace-level combined)
    - policy_type: trace_ql
      trace_ql:
        query: '{ resource.service.name = "payments" && duration > 500ms }'

    # Probabilistic fallback: 2% of everything else
    - policy_type: probabilistic
      probabilistic:
        sampling_percentage: 2

Consider a production agentic AI platform handling 5 000 agent invocations per minute. Each invocation produces 15–40 spans (LLM calls, tool calls, retrieval steps, memory reads/writes). At 40 spans per invocation, that is 200 000 spans/minute — roughly 3 300 spans/second. At 100% retention, at an average span size of 2 KB, storage accumulates at approximately 400 MB/minute or 576 GB/day. At a backend cost of $0.05/GB-month, the annual trace storage bill approaches $10 000 for traces alone, before query infrastructure.
The team deploys three tail-sampling collector instances behind a load-balancing collector. Their policy set: keep all traces where any span has
(typically ~2% of invocations); keep all traces where total root-span duration exceeds 10 seconds (another ~3%); keep all traces where the agent invoked the external billing tool (matched by

via a string attribute policy); and keep 0.5% of everything else as a statistical baseline. The result: approximately 6.5% of traces are retained. Storage drops to ~37 GB/day — a 93% reduction. Every error and every slow invocation is present. The billing tool's traces are always available for audit.

The collector tier runs three instances with 32 GB RAM each, configured with a 30-second decision window and a 50 000 concurrent-trace limit. CPU utilization at peak is 40%. A Kubernetes PodDisruptionBudget ensures at most one instance drains at a time during rollouts, preventing span loss during planned maintenance.
Using round-robin load balancing in front of tail samplers.

Without traceId-based routing, spans from the same trace scatter to different collector instances. The assembler never sees a complete trace and either TTL-fires on partial data or evaluates policies incorrectly. Always use the
Setting decision_wait shorter than the longest trace.
If an LLM call takes 25 seconds and
is 10 seconds, the trace is force-evaluated and possibly discarded before the slow span arrives. The resulting trace in the backend is incomplete or absent. Set
to at least 2× the p99 trace duration.
to be exceeded, causing the oldest buffered traces to be evicted and lost. Size memory for 2× peak concurrent traces, not average load.
Relying on tail sampling instead of metrics for alerting.

Tail sampling is for trace retention, not real-time monitoring. Error-rate alerts should come from metrics (counters, RED signals) — not from querying the trace backend, which has a 30-second buffer delay that makes it unsuitable for alerting.
Forgetting to exempt health-check and liveness endpoints.
at high frequency. Without an explicit drop policy, they consume buffer capacity and count against rate limiters, pushing the fallback probabilistic policy to fire more aggressively than intended.
Start with error and latency policies, then add specifics.

The error and latency policies cover 90% of the value. Add string attribute or TraceQL policies only for specific high-value workflows (billing, authentication, agent tool use) once baseline coverage is validated.
Export collector self-metrics to Prometheus. Monitor
. Alert if the drop rate spikes unexpectedly.
Test policies in shadow mode first.

Configure a second pipeline that sends all spans to a separate backend alongside the tail sampler. Compare what tail sampling would keep versus what is actually kept during the first week to validate policy coverage before depending on it.
Even 0.1% of healthy traces provides statistical insight into normal traffic shape, enabling baseline comparisons and detecting regressions that are slow but not threshold-crossing.
Sampling decisions are irreversible. Treat policy configuration as code: review changes in PRs, deploy via GitOps, and document the rationale for each policy alongside SLO thresholds.

The tail sampling collector is memory-bound, not CPU-bound. Each buffered span consumes roughly 1–5 KB in the in-memory map (depending on attribute cardinality). A collector buffering 50 000 concurrent traces with an average of 20 spans each, at 2 KB per span, holds approximately 2 GB of span data. Add OS and process overhead and the working set exceeds 3 GB. Provision at least 2× the expected peak working set to avoid OOM kills.

CPU is consumed primarily by policy evaluation (string matching, attribute lookups) and serialization when forwarding kept traces. Policy evaluation is O(policies × spans-per-trace) per trace. With five policies and 30 spans, that is 150 comparisons per trace — negligible individually, but at 10 000 traces/second it becomes 1.5 million comparisons/second. Simple policies (
) are faster than regex string matching. Avoid regular expressions in string attribute policies at high throughput; prefer exact-match lists instead.

Horizontal scaling is achieved by adding tail-sampling collector instances and updating the load balancer's backend list. The load-balancing exporter uses consistent hashing, so adding a new instance causes only

of traces to be re-routed (where N is the new instance count), not a full rehash. This minimizes trace breakage during scale-out events. During scale-in, drain instances gracefully by waiting one full
component — the reference implementation. Supported policy types include

(v2.3+) natively supports tail sampling with TraceQL-based policies evaluated server-side. Simplifies the architecture by collapsing the tail sampler into the Tempo distributor, reducing infrastructure components.
has a built-in adaptive sampling strategy (rate-feedback driven) but does not offer policy-level control comparable to the OTel processor. Generally used behind an OTel tail sampler.

is a purpose-built tail sampling proxy for the Honeycomb backend. Supports rule-based sampling with dynamic configuration reload and sampling-aware metrics without needing a full OTel collector stack.

performs server-side tail sampling at the Datadog intake, with priority-based rules that automatically retain error and slow traces. Configuration is through Datadog's sampling rules API rather than a self-hosted collector.
Very high volume, storage-cost constraints, uniform traffic patterns
High volume, error/latency critical, agentic AI systems
Centrally managed head sampling with per-service rates

Tail sampling collectors are stateful — a crash or OOM event loses every span currently in the buffer. Mitigate this by running at least two instances with Kubernetes pod anti-affinity to spread them across availability zones, setting resource limits to prevent OOM via eviction rather than kill, and enabling graceful shutdown drain so that on planned restarts the collector flushes buffered decisions before stopping. The loss window on an ungraceful crash is bounded by the
interval — typically 10–30 seconds of buffered spans.

All spans — including those that will eventually be dropped — pass through the buffer. If spans carry PII (user IDs, prompts, API keys in attributes), the buffer is a transient but real exposure surface. Apply attribute redaction via an OTel transform processor in the pipeline

the tail sampler, not after, so that even dropped spans never hold raw sensitive data in collector memory. Ensure collector nodes are covered by disk encryption and memory limits prevent swap to disk.

Sampling decisions are not reversible. If a policy silently drops a class of trace — for example, a string attribute policy that accidentally matches a billing endpoint — the evidence is gone permanently. Maintain sampling policies as version-controlled configuration (GitOps). Log policy changes and emit a sampling-decision metric broken down by policy name. Periodically replay a sample of production spans through the policy engine in a staging environment to verify coverage and catch regressions before they reach production.

Think of head sampling as a hospital triage desk that turns patients away at the door based solely on their appearance, before any examination. Most people turned away are fine, but some who look healthy turn out to be critically ill. Tail sampling is a different model: everyone is admitted, assessed after test results are back, and then either admitted to a ward or discharged. The triage step is moved to after the outcome is known, so the ward fills with people who actually need attention, not a random cross-section of everyone who walked through the door.
Head sampling makes uninformed decisions at trace start and statistically discards the same fraction of errors as healthy requests, making it unsuitable when error trace retention is critical.

Tail sampling defers the keep-or-drop decision until the trace is complete, allowing policies to guarantee retention of errors, latency outliers, and other high-value trace classes while discarding the healthy majority.

The tail sampling collector buffers all spans for each traceId in memory until the root span arrives or a TTL fires; this makes it memory-intensive and requires stateful, high-availability deployment with traceId-aware routing.

The OpenTelemetry Collector tailsamplingprocessor supports error-status, latency, rate-limiting, string-attribute, and composite policies; Grafana Tempo adds TraceQL-based policies evaluated at ingest without a separate collector tier.
Typical tail sampling configurations achieve 90–99% storage reduction compared to 100% retention, while retaining 100% of error and latency-outlier traces.

Tail sampling is not a substitute for metrics-based alerting: the buffer delay (10–30 seconds) makes trace queries unsuitable for real-time incident detection; use RED metrics for alerting and tail-sampled traces for diagnosis.

Sampling policy configuration must be version-controlled, monitored via collector self-metrics, and audited periodically — dropped spans are irrecoverable and a misconfigured policy silently destroys debug signal.
Apply PII redaction before the tail sampler in the pipeline, not after, so that sensitive attributes are never held in the buffer even for traces that will ultimately be discarded.
